questions.question:
1. Some football players play cricket.
2. All cricket players play hockey.

Among the options given below, the statement that logically follows from the two statements 1 and 2 above, is:

A. No football player plays hockey
B. Some football players play hockey
C. All football players play hockey
D. All hockey players play football
questions.answer: B. Some football players play hockey

understand.plain: We have two premises involving football players, cricket players, and hockey players. The first says some football players are also cricket players. The second says every cricket player is also a hockey player. We need to find what must be true as a logical consequence of combining these two facts. Any football player who plays cricket must also play hockey, and since some football players do play cricket, some football players must play hockey.

keywords.explain: In logic, 'some' means 'at least one exists'; it does not imply 'all'.
keywords.example: Some football players play cricket means at least one football player also plays cricket.

keywords.explain: In logic, 'all' means 'every member of the set without exception'.
keywords.example: All cricket players play hockey means there is no cricket player who does not play hockey.

keywords.explain: A form of deductive reasoning where a conclusion follows from two premises that share a common middle term.
keywords.example: Some A are B; All B are C; therefore Some A are C.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 340 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='120' cy='100' r='70' fill='#e8e4f8' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><circle cx='190' cy='100' r='55' fill='#d4f0e4' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><circle cx='230' cy='100' r='45' fill='#fde8e0' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.6'/><text x='85' y='90' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Football</text><text x='150' y='85' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Cricket</text><text x='245' y='90' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Hockey</text><text x='150' y='108' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>(some overlap)</text><text x='170' y='175' class='tag mut' text-anchor='middle'>Some football players play both cricket and hockey</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing football as a large circle, cricket as a smaller circle overlapping football (some), and hockey as a circle fully containing cricket (all cricket players play hockey), illustrating that the overlapping football-cricket region is also inside hockey.

given.aim: Determine which conclusion logically follows from the two given statements about football, cricket, and hockey players.

terms.meaning: Some football players play cricket — the sets 'football players' and 'cricket players' have a non-empty intersection.
terms.example: At least one person belongs to both groups.
terms.connects: Provides the partial overlap between football and cricket players.

terms.meaning: All cricket players play hockey — the set 'cricket players' is fully contained inside the set 'hockey players'.
terms.example: Every member of the cricket group is also a member of the hockey group.
terms.connects: Tells us that any cricket player is also a hockey player, bridging cricket and hockey.

terms.meaning: A conclusion that must be true whenever all the given premises are true.
terms.example: If some A are B and all B are C, then some A are C is a valid inference.
terms.connects: Allows us to chain the two statements to draw the conclusion about football and hockey.

given.plan: Identify football players who play cricket (from Statement 1). Apply Statement 2 to show those same players also play hockey. Conclude that some football players play hockey. Then verify the other options cannot be guaranteed.

steps.title: Identify the intersection from Statement 1
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))
steps.apply: Statement 1 says: Some football players play cricket. So there exists at least one person x such that x plays football AND x plays cricket.
steps.note: This is a non-empty intersection — at least one football player is also a cricket player.

steps.title: Apply Statement 2 to those players
steps.formula_raw: 'All A have property B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: Statement 2 says: All cricket players play hockey. So for every person x, if x plays cricket then x plays hockey. Applying this to the cricket-playing football player found in Step 1: that person also plays hockey.
steps.note: The universal statement covers every cricket player, including those who also play football.

steps.title: Combine to get the conclusion
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and A, conclude B
steps.apply: We have: some football player x plays cricket (Step 1). All cricket players play hockey (Step 2). Therefore x also plays hockey. Hence at least one football player plays hockey, which is option B: 'Some football players play hockey'.
steps.note: Options A (no football player plays hockey) is contradicted. Option C (all football players play hockey) is stronger than what we can infer — Statement 1 only says 'some' football players play cricket. Option D has no support.

solution.result: B. Some football players play hockey

questions.formula_note: The argument is a classic existential-universal syllogism: some A are B combined with all B are C yields some A are C.

questions.question:
Given below are two statements and four conclusions drawn based on the statements.

Statement 1: Some soaps are clean.
Statement 2: All clean objects are wet.

Conclusion I: Some clean objects are soaps.
Conclusion II: No clean object is a soap.
Conclusion III: Some wet objects are soaps.
Conclusion IV: All wet objects are soaps.

Which one of the following options is correct?

A. Only conclusion I is correct
B. Either conclusion I or conclusion II is correct
C. Either conclusion III or conclusion IV is correct
D. Both conclusions I and III are correct
questions.answer: D. Both conclusions I and III are correct

understand.plain: Two statements link soaps, clean objects, and wet objects. The first tells us some soaps are clean, meaning the intersection of soaps and clean objects is non-empty. The second tells us every clean object is wet, making 'clean' a subset of 'wet'. We must evaluate four conclusions and identify which are necessarily true. Conclusion I follows directly from Statement 1 by symmetry of 'some'. Conclusion III follows by chaining both statements. Conclusions II and IV are contradicted or unsupported.

keywords.explain: If some soaps are clean, then some clean objects are soaps — the 'some' relationship is symmetric.
keywords.example: Some A are B implies some B are A, because the same individuals witness both.

keywords.explain: Linking 'some A are B' and 'all B are C' yields 'some A are C'.
keywords.example: Some soaps are clean; all clean objects are wet; therefore some soaps are wet, equivalently some wet objects are soaps.

keywords.explain: Statement 1 directly says some clean objects ARE soaps, so 'No clean object is a soap' is false.
keywords.example: Once we know at least one clean soap exists, the universal negation is refuted.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='130' cy='100' r='70' fill='#e8e4f8' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><circle cx='195' cy='100' r='50' fill='#d4f0e4' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><circle cx='240' cy='100' r='65' fill='#fde8e0' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5'/><text x='90' y='95' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Soaps</text><text x='165' y='85' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Clean</text><text x='280' y='75' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Wet</text><text x='165' y='108' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>some overlap</text><text x='240' y='125' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Clean subset of Wet</text><text x='180' y='178' class='tag mut' text-anchor='middle'>I and III both follow</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram with Soaps as a large circle, Clean as a smaller circle overlapping Soaps (some soaps are clean), and Wet as the largest circle fully containing Clean (all clean are wet), showing the soap-clean overlap is also inside wet.

given.aim: Identify which of the four conclusions follow necessarily from the two given statements.

terms.meaning: There exists at least one object that is both a soap and a clean object.
terms.example: At least one soap also belongs to the 'clean' category.
terms.connects: Establishes a non-empty intersection between soaps and clean objects.

terms.term: Statement 2: All clean objects are wet
terms.meaning: The entire set of clean objects is contained in the set of wet objects.
terms.example: Every clean thing is also wet; no clean thing is dry.
terms.connects: Makes 'clean' a subset of 'wet', bridging clean and wet in the chain.

terms.meaning: If some A are B then some B are A, because the same witnessing element satisfies both.
terms.example: Some soaps are clean implies some clean objects are soaps (Conclusion I).
terms.connects: Validates Conclusion I directly from Statement 1.

given.plan: Check each conclusion. For I: apply symmetry to Statement 1. For II: check against Statement 1 directly. For III: chain both statements. For IV: check whether 'all wet are soaps' can be derived.

steps.title: Check Conclusion I: Some clean objects are soaps
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))
steps.apply: Statement 1 says: exists x (soap(x) and clean(x)). This same x satisfies clean(x) and soap(x). So some clean objects are soaps. Conclusion I is TRUE.
steps.note: The 'some' relation is symmetric: some A are B implies some B are A.

steps.title: Check Conclusion II: No clean object is a soap
steps.formula_raw: p -> q is False only when p is True and q is False
steps.apply: Conclusion I proved that some clean object IS a soap. Conclusion II directly contradicts this. Conclusion II is FALSE.
steps.note: Conclusions I and II cannot both hold; since I is proven true, II is false.

steps.title: Check Conclusion III: Some wet objects are soaps
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (B -> C), conclude (A -> C)
steps.apply: From Step 1: exists x (soap(x) and clean(x)). Statement 2: forall x (clean(x) -> wet(x)). Applying Statement 2 to x: wet(x) is true. So x is both a soap and wet. Therefore some wet objects are soaps. Conclusion III is TRUE.
steps.note: This is the classic syllogism chain: some A are B, all B are C, so some A are C, equivalently some C are A.

steps.title: Check Conclusion IV: All wet objects are soaps
steps.formula_raw: 'All A have property B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: Statement 2 says all clean objects are wet, but wet objects can be many things — tap water, rain, etc. There is no premise that restricts all wet objects to being soaps. Conclusion IV is unsupported and FALSE.
steps.note: We can only say some wet objects are soaps; we cannot say all wet objects are soaps.

solution.result: D. Both conclusions I and III are correct

questions.formula_note: The solution combines the symmetry of existential statements with the transitivity chain: some soaps are clean and all clean are wet yields some soaps (hence some wet objects) are in both groups.

questions.question:
A duck named Donald Duck says 'All ducks always lie.'

Based only on the information above, which one of the following statements can be logically inferred with certainty?

A. Donald Duck always lies.
B. Donald Duck always tells the truth.
C. Donald Duck's statement is true.
D. Donald Duck's statement is false.
questions.answer: D. Donald Duck's statement is false.

understand.plain: This is a self-referential liar-type paradox. Donald Duck, a duck, claims 'All ducks always lie.' If this statement were true, then Donald Duck (being a duck) always lies, which means the statement he just made is a lie — a contradiction. Because the statement cannot be true without producing a contradiction, the statement must be false. A false statement means the negation holds: not all ducks always lie, i.e., at least one duck does not always lie. Donald Duck could be that duck — he may sometimes tell the truth and sometimes lie. We cannot be certain he always lies or always tells the truth; we can only be certain his statement is false.

keywords.explain: A statement that refers to itself or to its own truth value, capable of producing paradoxes.
keywords.example: The Liar Paradox: 'This statement is false.' If true, it is false; if false, it is true.

keywords.explain: Assuming a claim is true and deriving a logical contradiction proves the claim must be false.
keywords.example: Assume 'All ducks lie' is true. Then Donald lies. So 'All ducks lie' is a lie — contradiction.

keywords.explain: The negation of 'All A are B' is 'Some A are not B' (existential negation).
keywords.example: Negation of 'All ducks always lie' is 'Some duck does not always lie'.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a self-referential logical argument involving a single statement and its negation.

given.aim: Determine what can be logically inferred with certainty from Donald Duck's statement 'All ducks always lie.'

terms.meaning: The universal statement: for every duck x, x always lies. Donald Duck himself is a duck.
terms.example: This applies to Donald himself, making the statement self-referential.
terms.connects: Creates the contradiction when we assume the statement is true.

terms.meaning: If 'All ducks always lie' is true, then Donald Duck, being a duck, always lies.
terms.example: But then the statement Donald just made ('All ducks always lie') must itself be a lie.
terms.connects: This means the statement is false — contradicting the assumption that it is true.

terms.meaning: Since assuming the statement is true leads to a contradiction, the statement must be false.
terms.example: The negation holds: not all ducks always lie. At least one duck sometimes tells the truth.
terms.connects: This is the only certain inference; we cannot determine whether Donald specifically always lies or always tells the truth.

given.plan: Assume the statement is true and derive a contradiction. Conclude the statement must be false. Check each option: A and B require certainty about Donald's behavior that we do not have; C is ruled out by the contradiction; D (statement is false) is the only certain conclusion.

steps.title: Assume Donald's statement is true
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false; otherwise it is true
steps.apply: Assume 'All ducks always lie' is TRUE. Donald Duck is a duck. Therefore Donald Duck always lies. But if Donald always lies, then every statement Donald makes is a lie. Donald just stated 'All ducks always lie' — that statement must therefore be a lie, meaning it is FALSE.
steps.note: We now have the statement being both TRUE (our assumption) and FALSE (from the derivation) — a contradiction.

steps.title: Conclude the statement is false
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (not B), conclude (not A)
steps.apply: Assuming the statement is true leads to a contradiction (it must then be false). By reductio ad absurdum, the assumption fails. Therefore the statement 'All ducks always lie' is FALSE.
steps.note: A false universal 'All ducks lie' means its negation is true: some duck does not always lie.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.formula_raw: ~for all x phi = exists x ~phi ; ~exists x phi = for all x ~phi
steps.apply: Option A (Donald always lies): The false statement only tells us not every duck always lies; it does not force Donald to always lie — NOT certain. Option B (Donald always tells truth): Similarly, we cannot confirm this — NOT certain. Option C (statement is true): Ruled out in Step 1. Option D (statement is false): Proven in Step 2 — CERTAIN.
steps.note: Only option D can be inferred with certainty from the self-referential contradiction.

solution.result: D. Donald Duck's statement is false.

questions.formula_note: The argument is a proof by contradiction: the self-referential universal statement, if true, implies its own falsehood, so it must be false.

questions.question:
Based only on the truth of the statement 'Some humans are intelligent', which one of the following options can be logically inferred with certainty?

A. No human is intelligent.
B. All humans are intelligent.
C. Some non-humans are intelligent.
D. Some intelligent beings are humans.
questions.answer: D. Some intelligent beings are humans.

understand.plain: The given statement is 'Some humans are intelligent', which is an existential statement asserting that the intersection of the set of humans and the set of intelligent beings is non-empty. From this single statement we need to find what follows with certainty. The key insight is that the 'some' relation is symmetric: if some humans are intelligent, then the same individuals are both human and intelligent, so some intelligent beings are also humans. Options A and B contradict or go beyond the given statement, and option C introduces non-humans which the statement says nothing about.

keywords.explain: A statement of the form 'Some A are B' asserts there is at least one element in the intersection of sets A and B.
keywords.example: 'Some humans are intelligent' means at least one person is both human and intelligent.

keywords.explain: If some A are B, then the witnessing element is also a B that is an A, so some B are A.
keywords.example: Some humans are intelligent implies some intelligent beings are humans (the same people).

keywords.explain: Beings that are not human; the given statement provides no information about whether any non-human is intelligent.
keywords.example: We cannot infer anything about animals or robots from a statement about humans only.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='110' cy='90' r='65' fill='#e8e4f8' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><circle cx='190' cy='90' r='65' fill='#d4f0e4' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><ellipse cx='150' cy='90' rx='25' ry='30' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.9'/><text x='75' y='80' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Humans</text><text x='225' y='80' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Intelligent</text><text x='150' y='88' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>some</text><text x='150' y='104' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>overlap</text><text x='150' y='158' class='tag mut' text-anchor='middle'>Some humans = some intelligent beings</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram of two overlapping circles: 'Humans' on the left and 'Intelligent' on the right, with the shaded overlap region representing the some humans who are intelligent, which are simultaneously some intelligent beings who are human.

given.aim: Determine what can be logically inferred with certainty from the single given statement 'Some humans are intelligent'.

terms.meaning: Some humans are intelligent: exists x such that human(x) and intelligent(x).
terms.example: At least one human being is intelligent.
terms.connects: This is the only premise; all inferences must follow solely from this.

terms.term: Option D — Some intelligent beings are humans
terms.meaning: exists x such that intelligent(x) and human(x) — the same existential witness works.
terms.example: The same human who is intelligent also qualifies as an intelligent being who is human.
terms.connects: This is the symmetric restatement of the given statement and follows with certainty.

terms.term: Option C — Some non-humans are intelligent
terms.meaning: exists x such that not-human(x) and intelligent(x).
terms.example: A dolphin being intelligent would satisfy this, but the given statement says nothing about non-humans.
terms.connects: Cannot be inferred because the given statement only mentions some humans; it is silent on non-humans.

given.plan: Apply the symmetry of the existential 'some' relation to confirm option D. Check options A and B against the given statement. Check option C for whether non-humans are mentioned. Select the one that follows with certainty.

steps.title: Formalize the given statement
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))
steps.apply: Given: Some humans are intelligent. In first-order logic: exists x (human(x) and intelligent(x)). Call this witnessing individual 'p' — p is human and p is intelligent.
steps.note: The statement is purely existential; it says nothing about humans who are NOT intelligent.

steps.title: Check option A: No human is intelligent
steps.formula_raw: ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x) and ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x)
steps.apply: Option A says: forall x (human(x) -> not intelligent(x)). But the given statement provides a counterexample p who is both human and intelligent. Option A is FALSE.
steps.note: A single counterexample refutes a universal negation.

steps.title: Check option B: All humans are intelligent
steps.formula_raw: p -> q is False only when p is True and q is False
steps.apply: Option B is a stronger claim (universal) than the given (existential). 'Some' does not imply 'all'. We have no information about humans who might not be intelligent. Option B CANNOT be inferred with certainty.
steps.note: Going from 'some' to 'all' is an invalid generalization.

steps.title: Check option C: Some non-humans are intelligent
steps.apply: The given statement is about humans only; it provides no information about non-humans. We cannot infer anything about non-humans from a statement about humans. Option C CANNOT be inferred with certainty.
steps.note: Introducing non-humans requires additional premises not provided.

steps.title: Confirm option D: Some intelligent beings are humans
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))
steps.apply: From the formalized statement: exists x (human(x) and intelligent(x)). The same x satisfies: intelligent(x) and human(x). Therefore: some intelligent beings are humans. Option D follows with certainty by the symmetry of conjunction.
steps.note: The conjunction 'A and B' is equivalent to 'B and A'; flipping the roles gives the same existential fact.

solution.result: D. Some intelligent beings are humans.

questions.formula_note: The core inference is the symmetry of existential conjunction: 'some A are B' and 'some B are A' are logically equivalent because both assert the same non-empty intersection.
